2:12:592 hours, 12 minutes, 59 secondscombining tables data modeling in Tableau each successful dashboard or charts in
2:13:072 hours, 13 minutes, 7 secondsTableau going to be based on a solid data model and having data modeling skills is essential for each Tableau project or business intelligence
2:13:152 hours, 13 minutes, 15 secondsprojects so that's why we're going to start learning the fundamentals of data modeling including the star schema and the snow Fleck schema and then I'm going to introduce you to the Tableau Data
2:13:242 hours, 13 minutes, 24 secondsmodeling where you're going to learn the physical and The Logical layers and then we're going to learn the different methods on how to combine tables in data
2:13:312 hours, 13 minutes, 31 secondsmodeling using joints Union relationships and data blending and of course in order to understand the differences between them we're going to compare them side by side and of course
2:13:392 hours, 13 minutes, 39 secondsI'm going to guide you in when to use which methods and at the end we're going to go and build two data sources based on our training data sets so let's start with the first topic where we're going
2:13:472 hours, 13 minutes, 47 secondsto understand the fundamentals of data modeling so now let's let's go in real projects your data going to
2:13:582 hours, 13 minutes, 58 secondsbe sted typically in data warehouses or data Lakes inside many many different tables and the first step in any visualization tools like Tableau or
2:14:062 hours, 14 minutes, 6 secondsBarbi is to connect those tables and combine them in one big data
2:14:142 hours, 14 minutes, 14 secondsmodel so let's start with the question what is data modeling data modelling is the process of organizing and
2:14:212 hours, 14 minutes, 21 secondsrepresenting data in a clear and understandable way each data model has entities entities could be things like
2:14:272 hours, 14 minutes, 27 secondscustomers and products or events like orders and inside those entities we have informations and we call them attributes
2:14:362 hours, 14 minutes, 36 secondslike the first name and the last name inside the entity customers and we describe in the data model how those entities are connected or related to
2:14:442 hours, 14 minutes, 44 secondseach others and we call it relationships this data model this visual representation of the data makes it easier for us and for programs to
2:14:532 hours, 14 minutes, 53 secondsunderstand the data which is really important for making decisions and improving performance of the
2:15:022 hours, 15 minutes, 2 secondsbusiness all right so we have three different types of data models at different levels of abstraction first we
2:15:092 hours, 15 minutes, 9 secondshave the conceptual data model this type is high level representation of the data model without going in details on how
2:15:162 hours, 15 minutes, 16 secondsthe data model is implemented it's like a map that shows the important entities and the relationships and we usually use this type to explain the data models to
2:15:252 hours, 15 minutes, 25 secondsbusiness analysts and stockholders to understand the big picture of the data the second type is The Logical data model in this data model we go more in
2:15:342 hours, 15 minutes, 34 secondsdetails on how the data is structured and organized we Define in this model the attributes of each entity and it includes as well constraints and more
2:15:432 hours, 15 minutes, 43 secondsdetails about the relationships between the entities this data model is usually used by database designers and developers as a blueprint for the
2:15:512 hours, 15 minutes, 51 secondsimplementations and the third type is the physical data model this type represents the actual implementations of the data model it includes all the
2:16:002 hours, 16 minutestechnical details about how to store the data like the data types of the attributes the primary and foreign Keys indexes and so on this data model is
2:16:092 hours, 16 minutes, 9 secondsused by developers to create and manage the databases all right so let's summarize the conceptual data model shows the big picture of the data The
2:16:172 hours, 16 minutes, 17 secondsLogical data model provide a blueprint for the implementation and the physical data model shows how the data is implemented in the databases
2:16:262 hours, 16 minutes, 26 secondsand Tableau did adopt both the logical and physical data models in the data sources but we don't have conceptual data model in Tableau don't worry about
2:16:332 hours, 16 minutes, 33 secondsit I will show you more details later all right so now for analytics and
2:16:412 hours, 16 minutes, 41 secondsespecially for data warehousing and business intelligence we need special data models that are optimized for queries and for analytics it should be

3:04:183 hours, 4 minutes, 18 secondsdifference between joins and unions both of them are very similar they're going to combine two tables in one big table but the difference here is that's how
3:04:263 hours, 4 minutes, 26 secondsthe data going to be combined in joints the fields of both tables going to be combined so we're going to take all the fields from the left side and beside it
3:04:333 hours, 4 minutes, 33 secondsall the fields from the right sides so the results we're going to get one big wild table but in the other hand in the unions two tables going to be combined
3:04:423 hours, 4 minutes, 42 secondsbut instead of combining the fields here we're going to combine the rows of both tables so we will get all the rows from the first table and beneath it all the
3:04:513 hours, 4 minutes, 51 secondsrows from the right table but both of them has exactly the same columns so joints combines the fields and Union combines the rows all right so that was

3:53:453 hours, 53 minutes, 45 secondsorigin story if you learn the concepts of data warehousing and business intelligence you might already know that the core concept is the
3:53:523 hours, 53 minutes, 52 secondsmulti-dimensional olab online analytical processing so the concept says if you want to answer the business questions or do data analyzes first we have to build
3:54:003 hours, 54 minutesa data model that has the shape of a cube with multi-dimensions it's something like this Cube and each Cube has two informations first we have the
3:54:093 hours, 54 minutes, 9 secondsdimensions of the cube and the second informations we have those cells those cells can store informations like data numbers and we call it measures so each
3:54:183 hours, 54 minutes, 18 secondsCube has two informations the dimensions and the cells the measures and now let's have an example we have the cube of sales and it has three dimensions the
3:54:273 hours, 54 minutes, 27 secondsFirst Dimension is the locations and inside the locations we have three members USA France and Germany those three values are the member of the

3:54:523 hours, 54 minutes, 52 secondscan start answering the business questions for example find the total sales in USA so what going to happen we can select the dimensional location and
3:55:003 hours, 55 minutesfilter the dimension to have only the member USA this operation in the cube we call it slicing the cube and then we're going to aggregate the measure and we
3:55:083 hours, 55 minutes, 8 secondswill get the total sales of 120 and if you have Cube we can do multiple operations like slicing dicing roll up
3:55:163 hours, 55 minutes, 16 secondsdrill down and beot so if you have such a cube we can do data analysis and find fast answers to the business questions so now to summarize Dimensions contain
3:55:253 hours, 55 minutes, 25 secondsqualitative values they usually describe something like the product name the product category customer location and
3:55:323 hours, 55 minutes, 32 secondswe use Dimensions to categorize filter and show the level of details and in the other hand we have the measures they
3:55:393 hours, 55 minutes, 39 secondscontain numeric quantitative values that can be measured like the name says and the measures unlike the dimensions they
3:55:463 hours, 55 minutes, 46 secondscan be aggregated all right so this might be still confusing and if you say you know
3:55:533 hours, 55 minutes, 53 secondswhat if I look to my data how do I decide whether it's a dimension or a measure so here is my decision- making process first I check the data type of
3:56:013 hours, 56 minutes, 1 secondthe field whether it is a number if the answer is no then this field is a dimension but if the answer is yes then we going to ask the next question does
3:56:093 hours, 56 minutes, 9 secondsit make sense to aggregate the values of the field like doing the sum calculation on the Val values or finding the average value if the answer is yes then it is a
3:56:183 hours, 56 minutes, 18 secondsmeasure but if the answer is no then it is a dimension so what this means all non-numeric fields are dimensions but
3:56:253 hours, 56 minutes, 25 secondsnot all numeric fields are measures that really depends on the questions whether it makes sense to aggregate the values if yes then it is a measure if no then
3:56:333 hours, 56 minutes, 33 secondsit's Dimension okay so now let's practice in order to understand the concept of dimensions and measures and how they work we will check our data sets and we going to assign each field

4:11:484 hours, 11 minutes, 48 secondswith 0 1 2 3 and so on so that means between 0 and 10 we have exactly 11 distinct values but with the continuous

4:12:344 hours, 12 minutes, 34 secondsyou will just see more of the yarn so you will just get a longer piece of the same string all right so discrete values are separated distinct values and

5:30:075 hours, 30 minutes, 7 secondsand we call them centroids and then it going to assign the data points to the nearest centroid so for this data point it going to belong to the green cluster
5:30:155 hours, 30 minutes, 15 secondsand then it going to go to the next data point and calculate the length between it and the three centroids and then it's going to assign it to the nearest centroid for this it's going to be the
5:30:235 hours, 30 minutes, 23 secondsred cluster so the algorithm going to do that for all data points and assign them to the nearest centroid so at the end we're going to have three clusters the

6:14:236 hours, 14 minutes, 23 secondsreducing the size of such a data source going to mean reducing the processing time inside Tableau which going to lead to optimize response time in your
6:14:316 hours, 14 minutes, 31 secondsvisualizations so this is one of the reasons why we use filters in tableau to optimize the performance of our dashboards the Second Use case of
6:14:386 hours, 14 minutes, 38 secondsfilters is interactivity and analyzes we usually offer a set of different filters for the users because different users may have different goals or may be
6:14:476 hours, 14 minutes, 47 secondsinterested in specific aspect of the data so that means allowing the users to filter and to focus on subsets of the data going to help in better analyzing
6:14:566 hours, 14 minutes, 56 secondsand understanding of the data and the third use case for the filters is hiding sensitive informations data securing is becoming very important topic in each
6:15:046 hours, 15 minutes, 4 secondsproject as now many people are working with the data the data security is becoming a very important topic and in Tableau we can use filters to restrict
6:15:126 hours, 15 minutes, 12 secondsthe sensitive data or to hide it from the viewers to make sure that we are protecting such a sensitive or confidential data from being exposed to
6:15:206 hours, 15 minutes, 20 secondsthe others and the fourth use case for the filters is data Access Control Ro level security RLS so this means that we
6:15:276 hours, 15 minutes, 27 secondscan use the filters in tblo to limit the access to data of the users based on the role and the permissions because in real
6:15:356 hours, 15 minutes, 35 secondsproject you cannot just go and build visualizations and share it with everyone instead you have to protect your data and to have some data rest structions like for example you're going
6:15:436 hours, 15 minutes, 43 secondsto have users like sales employee they should not see the data like managers so in order to protect your data and implement the RO level security in

6:17:086 hours, 17 minutes, 8 secondsdifferent stations and if you are not using any type of Tableau filters the whole amount of data going to be moved and processed from one layer to another
6:17:166 hours, 17 minutes, 16 secondslayer so for example and those are just numbers to explain the concepts we have in the original source of our data 30,000 records that means the whole
6:17:246 hours, 17 minutes, 24 secondsamount of data going to be exist as well at the data source level so there we're going to have as well the same number of recards 30k and then the same amount
6:17:326 hours, 17 minutes, 32 secondsmight be as well the results of your queries so we're going to have as well 30k records in the visualizations so we might be in situation where the source
6:17:406 hours, 17 minutes, 40 secondsof our data might has a lot of unnecessary data so it's going to be really wasting resources and performance in Tableau if we are going to process
6:17:486 hours, 17 minutes, 48 secondsthe whole amount of data in each layer so what we're going to do we're going to go and apply different types of filters as your data is moving from left to
6:17:566 hours, 17 minutes, 56 secondsright from the sources to the visualizations the first type of filters
6:18:036 hours, 18 minutes, 3 secondsthat we can use code called the extract filter you can apply the extract filter between the source of your data and the Tableau Data Source you can use this
6:18:126 hours, 18 minutes, 12 secondstype of filter if you are using the extract connection so that means you cannot use the extract filter for the data sources using the live connection

6:18:456 hours, 18 minutes, 45 secondstaking very long time here usually we go and create the extract filter in order to get R of all unnecessary data and
6:18:526 hours, 18 minutes, 52 secondsremove it before it even enter Tableau and another benefit is optimizing the performance of your visualizations because we're going to have less data
6:19:006 hours, 19 minutesless processing time in Tableau and that's going to result in better response time time in your visualizations so the main purpose of
6:19:076 hours, 19 minutes, 7 secondsextract filter is to optimize both the loading time and as well the response

6:19:316 hours, 19 minutes, 31 secondssending everything the whole data but instead here the data Source can to filter the data first and then send the results so here instead of sending 20K

6:21:186 hours, 21 minutes, 18 secondsgoing to get the data from this timberl new table or subset and here the downside of the context filter is we are losing performance because tblo can

6:21:436 hours, 21 minutes, 43 secondsextra table well the answer for that is flexibility because once you apply a data source filter you are filtering all the worksheets that are connected to

7:16:337 hours, 16 minutes, 33 secondsthe first tip that I have for you is to utilize those filters so the extract filter data source filter and the context filter I saw a lot of projects
7:16:417 hours, 16 minutes, 41 secondswhere developers really forget about them or ignore them because they are not really important in the visualizations but they are very important for
7:16:497 hours, 16 minutes, 49 secondsoptimizing the performance in Tableau so my advice here is for you to always have a discussion with the end users about promoting one of those filters that you
7:16:577 hours, 16 minutes, 57 secondshave in visualizations to be first an extract filter if it cannot be an extract filter then the data source filter and the last option to optimize
7:17:057 hours, 17 minutes, 5 secondsthe performance is to bring it as a context filter because sometimes in the visualizations you really don't need all the data you don't need like for example
7:17:137 hours, 17 minutes, 13 seconds10 years of data in the visualizations so try to discuss it with the users to say maybe let's bring only two years of data to the visualizations and then you
7:17:237 hours, 17 minutes, 23 secondscan utilize an extract filter or data source filter in your workbook which can has a great impact on the performance
7:17:307 hours, 17 minutes, 30 secondsoverall in so don't forget or ignore those three filters the second filter tip that I
7:17:387 hours, 17 minutes, 38 secondshave for you is about optimizing the performance in Tableau which is avoid using only relevant values in your quick filters so for example if we go to the
7:17:467 hours, 17 minutes, 46 secondssubcategory over here we can see that it is currently set to only relevant values if you use this option for all your quick filters what can happen the
7:17:557 hours, 17 minutes, 55 secondsperformance in tblo going to be really bad and everything going to be really slow so we can go and switch it to something else like all values in
7:18:027 hours, 18 minutes, 2 secondsdatabase or in context so we can go and switch that and with that you're going to reduce the stress on the memory and
7:18:097 hours, 18 minutes, 9 secondsthe resources in Tableau but let's understand why all right so now let's understand what going to happen in Tableau if you're using your filters all
7:18:167 hours, 18 minutes, 16 secondsvalues in database or in context it's the same so once the viewers or the users start the reports if you're going to send only one query to the data
7:18:257 hours, 18 minutes, 25 secondssource and the data source going to answer with the results pack so that means we're going to have only one initial query as the user starts the
7:18:337 hours, 18 minutes, 33 secondsview but in the other hand if you are using only relevant values what can happen the view going to keep sending
7:18:407 hours, 18 minutes, 40 secondsqueries after query to the data source always to get an update and refresh in the view so that means the view going to
7:18:487 hours, 18 minutes, 48 secondskeep sending multiple queries for each user interactions which can to really impact the performance in Tableau because each time the users is clicking
7:18:567 hours, 18 minutes, 56 secondssomething or interacting with the view the view going to keep sending queries to the data source to get an update about the interaction which going to use
7:19:047 hours, 19 minutes, 4 secondsa lot of resources and memory in Tableau and going to slow everything down because each time the user is clicking something in the view or and interacting
7:19:127 hours, 19 minutes, 12 secondsThe View going to keep sending queries to the data source which going to consumes a lot of memory and resourc from Tableau and it's going to slow
7:19:197 hours, 19 minutes, 19 secondseverything down so be careful with your quick filters if you're having everything on only relevant values things might be slow so if the users are
7:19:277 hours, 19 minutes, 27 secondssuffering from Bad performance in Tableau maybe think about switching all those filters to all values in context or in the
7:19:397 hours, 19 minutes, 39 secondsdatabase I have another filter tip about optimizing the performance in Tableau which is avoid using Dimensions with
7:19:457 hours, 19 minutes, 45 secondshigh cardinality as quick filters those Dimensions might impact the performance in Tableau but first let's understand what is cardinality so cardinality is
7:19:547 hours, 19 minutes, 54 secondsthe number of distinct values in a field for example in our database we have the customer ID we have around 8 00 customer
7:20:017 hours, 20 minutes, 1 secondID and we have a lot of products names so those two Fields considered to be high cardinality dimensions in the other
7:20:097 hours, 20 minutes, 9 secondshand we have another dimensions for example the category we have only three values or the countries in our database we have only four countries and the
7:20:177 hours, 20 minutes, 17 secondssubcategory as well we have only 17 subcategories those Dimensions considered to be a low cality and if you are using them the performance going to
7:20:257 hours, 20 minutes, 25 secondsbe okay but if you are start using those Dimensions with high cality the performance might be bad so the best practice here is to avoid using High
7:20:347 hours, 20 minutes, 34 secondscardinality all right so back to our quick filters in our view as you can see the category and the subcategory there are dimensions with low cality so it's
7:20:427 hours, 20 minutes, 42 secondsfine to leave it at the view but the product name it has a lot of values it is Dimensions with high cality and it's really worth to discuss it with the
7:20:507 hours, 20 minutes, 50 secondsusers whether they really need such a filter in the view and if you find out no one needs it just remove it from The View just to have a good performance at
7:20:587 hours, 20 minutes, 58 secondstableau now let's move to the next filter tip is that let's say that the users really
7:21:067 hours, 21 minutes, 6 secondswant to see the product name or the customer ID any Dimension with high credity in the view so here the tip is
7:21:147 hours, 21 minutes, 14 secondsto change the filter modes so instead of having a drop- down list or a list we can use a wild match for Dimensions with
7:21:227 hours, 21 minutes, 22 secondshigh cardinality so why having a list of all the products or the customers in the view is a bad thing in Tableau or bad for the performance well each time
7:21:317 hours, 21 minutes, 31 secondsTableau has to go to the data source or to the database and prepare a distinct list of all the customers or all the products to be presented in the view so
7:21:397 hours, 21 minutes, 39 secondsinstead of having a list we could go and change it to Wild Card match and as you can see tblo is not preparing anything so we don't have any values to be
7:21:477 hours, 21 minutes, 47 secondspresented in the view only if the customers start interacting with the quick filter then after that t going to
7:21:547 hours, 21 minutes, 54 secondsgo to the database and brings the relevant values and with that we are avoiding using a lot of resources and unnecessary calculations in Tableau so
7:22:047 hours, 22 minutes, 4 secondsif you have a Dimensions with high cality either avoid using it or if you want to use it just use the Wild Card
7:22:147 hours, 22 minutes, 14 secondsmatch all right so let's move to the next p practice in Tableau is as well about optimizing the performance in Tableau which is start using the apply
7:22:227 hours, 22 minutes, 22 secondsbuttom in your quick filters because if you don't use it let me show you what's going to happen each time I'm deselecting something it is like a query
7:22:317 hours, 22 minutes, 31 secondssent to the data source so this is one query second query third query fourth query and so on so each time I'm
7:22:387 hours, 22 minutes, 38 secondsclicking on my filters there will be generated a lot of query to the data source which is consuming a lot of performance so instead of having such a
7:22:467 hours, 22 minutes, 46 secondsfilter we can customize and add the apply button so as we learned before we can go over here then customize and show
7:22:537 hours, 22 minutes, 53 secondsapply button so now as I'm clicking on those values in the filter no query is generated to the data source so we are
7:23:007 hours, 23 minutesnot using any resources in Tableau and once I'm done selecting what I need then I'm going to hit okay or apply what going to happen one query going to send
7:23:097 hours, 23 minutes, 9 secondsto the data source to bring the result to the view so with that we are reducing the number of queries that our visualizations is generating a tableau
7:23:177 hours, 23 minutes, 17 secondswhich is really great for the performance so my recommendation here if you have a filter like the subcategory or a dimension with high cality where
7:23:257 hours, 23 minutes, 25 secondsyou are using a list use apply buttom because the users will not select only one value they usually select multiple values and then at the end they can
7:23:337 hours, 23 minutes, 33 secondsapply but a filter like the category we have only three values like it doesn't Worth to use apply buttom it's only three so the user is going to maximum
7:23:427 hours, 23 minutes, 42 secondslike generate three queries so it's fine to not use apply buttom with the dimensions with really low cardinality so with the high cardinality or medium
7:23:507 hours, 23 minutes, 50 secondscardinality like the subcategory go and use apply button all right the next filter tip
7:23:587 hours, 23 minutes, 58 secondsthat we have is as well about the performance in Tableau which is avoid using exclude and always use include if it is possible so for example if we go
7:24:077 hours, 24 minutes, 7 secondsto the subcategory we have here the option of using include or exclude so if you're using exclude values those queries that are going to be generated
7:24:157 hours, 24 minutes, 15 secondsin Tableau are more complex than include more complex means more resources and might slow down the report or the view
7:24:237 hours, 24 minutes, 23 secondsin Tableau so avoid using exclude when it's possible so I'm going to switch it back to include which has better performance
7:24:337 hours, 24 minutes, 33 secondsall right so let's move to the next one and I promise you this is the last one about the performance which is minimize the number of Quick filters in your view
7:24:417 hours, 24 minutes, 41 secondsthose quick filters going to take not only the space in the view but also going to generate a lot of queries a lot of stress going to bring the whole
7:24:487 hours, 24 minutes, 48 secondsperformance in t down so try to avoid using a lot of quick filters and discuss with your users each time they need new filters whether it's really necessary to
7:24:577 hours, 24 minutes, 57 secondsput it in the view because I saw a lot of of roject Dos the users always wants a lot of filters so try to discuss them
7:25:057 hours, 25 minutes, 5 secondsand not always bringing a new quick filter to the dblo because you're going to end up having really bad performance in the view and no one going to be happy
7:25:137 hours, 25 minutes, 13 secondshaving bad response time in the visualizations so try to minimize the number of Quick filters in Tableau so
7:25:207 hours, 25 minutes, 20 secondsthat everyone is happy so now let's bring more filters to our view we're going to go for example

7:26:287 hours, 26 minutes, 28 secondshierarchy so our hierarchy starts for example with the country as a higher level than the city so start always with the higher level then move down to the

7:32:587 hours, 32 minutes, 58 seconds10 years of data the visualizations so try to discuss it with the users to say maybe let's bring only two years of data

7:33:217 hours, 33 minutes, 21 secondsfilters the second filter tip that I have for you is about optimizing the performance in Tableau which is avoid using only relevant values in your quick

7:33:597 hours, 33 minutes, 59 secondstableau if you're using your filters all values in database or in context it's the same so once the viewers or the

7:34:147 hours, 34 minutes, 14 secondsmeans we're going to have only one initial query as the user starts the view but in the other hand if you are using only relevant values what can
7:34:237 hours, 34 minutes, 23 secondshappen the view going to keep sending queries after query to the data source always to get an update refresh in the
7:34:307 hours, 34 minutes, 30 secondsview so that means the view going to keep sending multiple queries for each user interactions which can really impact the performance in Tableau
7:34:397 hours, 34 minutes, 39 secondsbecause each time the users is clicking something or interacting with the view the view going to keep sending queries to the data source to get an update
7:34:477 hours, 34 minutes, 47 secondsabout the interaction which going to use a lot of resources and memory in Tableau and going to slow everything down because each time the user is clicking

7:35:497 hours, 35 minutes, 49 secondsso those two Fields considered to be high cardinality dimensions in the other hand we have another dimensions for example the category we have only three

13:16:1813 hours, 16 minutes, 18 secondscalculations that we have in Tableau the aggregate calculations and I split the functions into two groups the first group going to aggregate the measures in
13:16:2613 hours, 16 minutes, 26 secondsour data source so we have the sum average count and so on and the second group where we can aggregate the dimensions of our data source and here
13:16:3413 hours, 16 minutes, 34 secondswe have only one function we have the attrib so now we're going to focus on the first group how to aggregate the measures in
13:16:4413 hours, 16 minutes, 44 secondsTableau all right so the first question is what are aggregate calculations in Tableau if you use those calculations you're going to aggregate the rows of
13:16:5213 hours, 16 minutes, 52 secondsthe data source and put the result at the visualizations level of the details so that means the dimension that you are using in the view going to control the
13:17:0113 hours, 17 minutes, 1 secondgranularity of the measure let's have a quick example in order to understand it so let's say that we have the orders table inside our data source and we
13:17:0913 hours, 17 minutes, 9 secondswould like to find the total sales by the products and in this example the sales is a measure and product is the dimension so in order to find the total
13:17:1813 hours, 17 minutes, 18 secondssales we're going to use the function sum in Tableau so it going to look like this we going to use the sum of sales and in the view we're going to have one
13:17:2613 hours, 17 minutes, 26 secondsdimension the product it is the one going to control the level of details in the view and then we have the result of the function sum so we're going to put
13:17:3413 hours, 17 minutes, 34 secondshere the results of the aggregations so now with this table we're going to go and group up the rows of the orders by the products so as you can see the first
13:17:4313 hours, 17 minutes, 43 secondsgroup is based on the product number one then we have the second group for the product number two three and four so as you can see the orders now is divided
13:17:5013 hours, 17 minutes, 50 secondsinto groups and at the visualization levels we going to have exactly only one row for each group so that means for the product one we're going to have only one
13:17:5913 hours, 17 minutes, 59 secondsrow and then tblo going to go and summarize all the sales inside this group so at the end in the result we can have the value of 40 so as you can see
13:18:0713 hours, 18 minutes, 7 secondsthe aggregate calculations is grouping up the rows from the data source and present it as one row at the output in
13:18:1513 hours, 18 minutes, 15 secondsthe visualizations then tblo going to move to the next group for the pay2 we're going to have only one row and the summarizations of the sales going to be 50 and the same thing going to happen

14:28:0414 hours, 28 minutes, 4 secondsof details it's going to be depending on the visualizations so that means here again the dimensions in the view going to control the level of details and now

14:28:5214 hours, 28 minutes, 52 secondscalculation going to query the data in the view all right guys in order to create

14:50:0414 hours, 50 minutes, 4 secondsokay guys so how the running total calculation works it's going to go and add each value to the sum of all previous values let's have an example in
14:50:1214 hours, 50 minutes, 12 secondsorder to understand it we have over here the months and the sales as well and we want to build the running sum so we start with the first value so we are
14:50:2014 hours, 50 minutes, 20 secondscurrently at the first row and since we don't have any previous sum of values it's going to be exactly the same value
14:50:2714 hours, 50 minutes, 27 secondsso the calculation going to be the current running total going to equal to the sales value so that means in the output we're going to get exactly the
14:50:3414 hours, 50 minutes, 34 secondssame value 2,67 moving on to the next month to the February so currently we are at this level at the sales 523 and
14:50:4314 hours, 50 minutes, 43 secondsthe previous running total going to be the old one from January so now in order to get the running total for February it's going to be simply adding those two
14:50:5114 hours, 50 minutes, 51 secondsvalues so we are adding the sales Value Plus the previous total run and with that we will get
14:50:5814 hours, 50 minutes, 58 seconds2590 so as you can see we are simply adding the current sales with the previous running value so let's move to the next months we have a new current we

14:51:2214 hours, 51 minutes, 22 secondsproceed and progress our table until we reach the last one it's going to be exactly the same so we are currently at December and this is our current value
14:51:3014 hours, 51 minutes, 30 secondswe're going to go and add it to the previous running total from the previous month November until we going to get the last value and with that we have the
